So what was the point of all that planning and development if only one ad gets shown? You're anticipating a need for all those ads, but there's a much smarter and more efficient approach. Just get started. Start small. Start with a single creative direction. Something I've been pushing lately is creative diversification at a small scale. One customer persona with one messaging goal, five primary text and headline options. You could create two ads, one using images and one using videos. Or you could create a single ad and customized by placement to use nine by 16 videos for reels and stories and static images everywhere else. You could also make use of flexible ad format, which Meta loves and provide up to 10 images or videos for single ad. But instead of mapping out all these various approaches with 10 or 20 or 30 ads, you start with a small handful. It could be one or two. And you hit publish. You just get started. And then you sit back and you see what happens. The chances are high that these initial ads won't be perfect. Are your results good enough? If so, let it ride for a while. small set of ads, your next creative direction. And I strongly recommend utilizing Meta's creative testing tool when you publish that next set. This is something I'm making part of my process now. By doing this, you force a percentage of your budget on those new ads during the test. And you keep those test ads within your main ad set. The main thing is that you're doing this in stages. You may end up with 20 or 30 ads, but you're not doing it all once. And you're not anticipating the entire set of creative from the start and slowing everything down in the process. You're starting small, hitting publish quickly, learning from results, and making incremental improvements. You can apply this to your overall campaign construction too. Advertisers often get lost in the weeds of planning new campaigns, making them far too complicated from the beginning.
